result:
The first thing that comes to me is the sheer, vertiginous weight of those words. A thousand. It’s not a casual handful. It’s a vessel, a container of specific volume. I could fill it with sand, or with diamonds, or with a single, perfect, breath-held silence stretched so thin it becomes transparent. The obligation of the blank space is always a kind of summoning. What will I conjure? What ghosts of thought, still half-formed in the ether, will take shape here?

Outside my window, the late afternoon light is doing that thing it does in early autumn—it’s not the gold of summer, but a clearer, more poignant yellow, like the taste of a Williams pear. It slants across the wooden floor, illuminating a million dancing dust motes. They aren’t dirt, in this light; they are galaxies, swirling in a sunbeam. This is the unrecorded poetry of an ordinary Tuesday. It happens and is gone, and no one but me, and perhaps the cat blinking slowly on the windowsill, bears witness. A thousand words could be spent just on that light, on the cat’s fur where it becomes a burning halo, on the quiet hum of the refrigerator, a modern-day hearth.

But then my mind, a restless creature, leaps away. It goes to the cold, dark soil of a forest I walked in last week. The smell of damp earth and rotting leaves, a scent so profound it feels less like smelling and more like drinking. The memory of my boot sinking into a patch of moss, a green so vibrant it seemed to pulse with its own private life. I thought then: *This is the world’s original carpet. This is what softness first meant.* That moment, too, is a claimant. It wants its hundred words, its two hundred. It wants to be remembered.

And then, the human tide rushes in. The friend who called last night, her voice cracking over a loss I cannot fix. The way we sat in that shared silence over the phone, a silence that was its own language. The weight of her grief was a tangible thing in my ear, and all I could offer was the anchor of my listening. A thousand words could easily be spent trying to dissect that silence, to find the grammar of comfort, and failing. Some things are held best in the spaces between syllables.

Perhaps I should use this space wisely. Should I argue a point? Champion a cause? Lay out a logical sequence, point A to point B, building a neat intellectual edifice? But my spirit rebels. The world is full of arguing. It is thick with takes and hot takes and burning opinions. What feels scarce, sometimes, is simple, undirected noticing. The sacrament of attention.

I think of Mary Oliver’s instruction: “Pay attention. Be astonished. Tell about it.”

So here is my astonishment, today: It is in the sheer, mad improbability of existence. That out of the formless fire of the Big Bang, over eons so vast they numb the mind, particles arranged themselves into stars, and planets, and then—somehow—into the intricate machinery of a maple seed, a beetle’s wing, the labyrinth of a human brain that can contemplate its own origin. My own hand, typing this: a colony of billions of cells, a history of evolution written in bone and sinew, capable of both a surgeon’s precision and a lover’s caress. It is obscenely miraculous. We walk through our days on a tapestry of wonders so dense we become blind to it. We are breathing stardust, thinking thunderstorms.

And then, the shadow. For every beam of light, a length of darkness. The astonishment is also in the capacity for cruelty, for the small, cold betrayals, for the vast, engineered indifference we can show one another. The same brain that composes a symphony can justify a genocide. The same heart that swells at a child’s laughter can harden into a stone to throw. This duality is the central drama. We are angels apprenticed to apes, forever torn between mud and music.

The light in the room is fading now. The galactic dust motes have vanished. The cat has shifted, a comma of fur curled into a full stop. The hum of the world continues. I have used some of my words—I can feel the container slowly filling. But have I said anything? Or have I simply traced the pattern of my own consciousness, like a finger on a foggy window?

Maybe that’s all we can ever do. Leave a record of our attention. A whisper against the void: *I was here. I saw the light on the floor. I felt the grief of my friend. I wondered at the moss and the stars. For a brief, flickering moment, I was awake and astonished.*

The project of a life, of a thousand words, isn’t to conclude. It’s to connect. To draw a line, however faint, between the observing self and the bewildering, beautiful, brutal world. To say: this light, this loss, this leaf, this love—they mattered to me. And in the telling, perhaps they ripple out, a pebble dropped in a dark pond, and touch another consciousness. Your consciousness, reading this now.

The thousand words are almost spent. The vessel is nearly full. What’s left is just the final silence, the unspoken word that hangs after the last period. The invitation. Now it’s your turn. Look out of your window. What do you see? What comes to you? The world is waiting, endlessly, for our astonished, aching, grateful testimony. Go on. You have your own thousand words, in a hundred forms, every single day. Use them.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
